column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'legacy-supported', 'notes': 'EDI remains in widespread use in B2B supply chain and retail, but is a mature/legacy standard. Still actively used in 95%+ of large-company supply chains, particularly in retail and healthcare, though newer standards (APIs, XML) are growing.', 'source': 'General knowledge / industry consensus'}
column9: Used for B2B purchase orders and invoices in supply chain.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'evolved', 'notes': 'Internet e-commerce as a concept has thoroughly evolved and matured into the dominant retail channel. US e-commerce sales reached ~$1.2 trillion in 2024 (16.2% of total retail), a fully evolved mainstream channel.', 'source': 'https://www.emarketer.com/content/us-ecommerce-forecast-2024'}
column9: Web-based buying/selling platforms.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'evolved', 'notes': 'Traditional on-premises data warehousing has evolved into cloud data warehousing (Snowflake, BigQuery, Redshift). The concept is active and growing but has fundamentally evolved in architecture.', 'source': 'General knowledge / industry consensus'}
column9: Decision support for supply chain demand anticipation.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'evolved', 'notes': 'Traditional intranet technology has evolved into modern digital workplace platforms (SharePoint, Teams, Slack, etc.). Enterprise intranet market valued at ~$45B in 2024, growing at 8.2% CAGR. Concept active; technology evolved.', 'source': 'https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/enterprise-intranet-software-market-digital-platform-1xeqc'}
column9: Internal networks connecting employees and workgroups.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Relational databases remain the dominant data storage paradigm. MySQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle DB, SQL Server all actively developed and widely deployed.', 'source': 'General knowledge / industry consensus'}
column9: Universal databases supporting searchable new media.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'legacy-unsupported', 'notes': 'Distributed objects technology (CORBA/ORB/DCOM) has been largely superseded by REST APIs, microservices, and gRPC. CORBA spec still exists via OMG but is not actively developed for mainstream use. Legacy installations remain.', 'source': 'https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Common_Object_Request_Broker_Architecture'}
column9: Fast-evolving and imprecise at time of writing; key architectural component.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Java remains one of the most widely used programming languages. Java 21 (LTS) and Java 23 actively supported. Widely used in enterprise backends, Android, and large-scale systems.', 'source': 'General knowledge'}
column9: 4GL/CASE tools generating Java cited as EC development tools.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'evolved', 'notes': 'Payment authentication and encryption has evolved substantially from early SSL/SET protocols to modern TLS 1.3, 3DS2, tokenization, and PCI-DSS frameworks. The domain is active but the 1990s-era specific implementations are superseded.', 'source': 'https://www.sectigo.com/blog/tls-client-authentication-public-ca-end-2026'}
column9: Credit card processing, 3rd party authorization, electronic cash.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'legacy-unsupported', 'notes': 'Object Request Brokers (CORBA ORB) are functionally obsolete. Replaced by REST, gRPC, and message queues. No mainstream ORB products are actively developed. Legacy enterprise systems may still use them.', 'source': 'https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Common_Object_Request_Broker_Architecture'}
column9: Middleware / CGI replacements cited.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'legacy-supported', 'notes': 'CGI (Common Gateway Interface) is technically functional and still used on some legacy servers, but has largely been replaced by FastCGI, WSGI, and modern server-side frameworks. Still supported by Apache/Nginx but not recommended for new development.', 'source': 'https://blog.apolocloud.net/cgi-common-gateway-interface-the-power/'}
column9: Being replaced by newer middleware at time of study.
Non-Uniform Memory Access architecture for scalable enterprise servers; concept now standard in all modern multi-socket x86 servers.
Multiple CPUs sharing common bus, memory, and OS; well understood but limited scalability beyond 4-10 processors in the 1990s.
Multiple independent nodes cooperating via message-passing; used for HA and resource sharing but had software bottleneck issues in 1996.
Intel's economical microprocessor platform that Sequent leveraged for enterprise superservers.
DEC's pioneering cluster technology for VAX systems; referenced as an example of traditional clustering.
IBM's Scalable POWERparallel system for high-performance computing; referenced as a cluster example.
IBM mainframe clustering technology; evolved into Parallel Sysplex which remains in use on modern z-series.
Core enterprise workload type; one of the primary targets for Sequent's NUMA-Q architecture.
Analytical workloads for complex queries; evolved into modern business intelligence and analytics platforms.
Asynchronous Transfer Mode; predicted to deliver cheap infinite bandwidth by 2001, but was superseded by Gigabit Ethernet.

Full enterprise management suite; $1B+ annual revenue run rate by 1997; 7,000+ customers, 200+ ISV partners, 37 OS platforms. Evolved into CA Technologies Unified Infrastructure Management. Now part of Broadcom.

Free/bundled subset of Unicenter TNG announced July 14, 1997; includes auto-discovery, object repository, 2D/3D mapping, event management, scheduling, virus scanning. 10-minute installation. 20,000 CDs distributed at CA World.
Enables ISVs and sophisticated IT orgs to build custom application management solutions using Framework as foundation.

CA's primary competitor; Kastner notes TME requires remote-network-supplied configuration estimates and 'days spent in network and asset evaluation' vs. CA's 10-minute install. Evolved into IBM Tivoli suite.
HP OpenView was dominant network management platform; Aberdeen says HP 'has clearly lost momentum' in 1997; HP's own systems division endorsed Unicenter Framework as preferred management platform.
Framework uses SNMP agents for monitoring; referenced in Unicenter TNG auto-discovery and event management.

Kastner describes 3D mapping as 'animation and elements of virtual reality to create realistic floor plans indicating location of devices within the building'; considered breakthrough enterprise management UI in 1997.
Framework initially operates on Windows NT Server; Unix versions promised for later summer 1997. NT compared to Unicenter TNG's $1B revenue run rate in Kastner's analysis.
DynaFlo's flagship product; Internet email for ISPs targeting non-early-adopter businesses; did not survive ISP consolidation
Internal LAN email systems the report describes as standard for businesses in 1997
Primary Internet access method in 1997; superseded by broadband from early 2000s
The model of ISPs offering value-added services beyond connectivity evolved into hosted cloud services, SaaS email etc.
Hardware-based fault-tolerant NT server; ~$24,995; two lock-stepped Pentium Pro CEs + two IOPs; 99.99% uptime; 1.5km fiber separation. Superseded by software-based everRun in 2004.
NT Server 4.0 released July 1996; growing enterprise adoption but lacked built-in fault tolerance. Superseded by Windows Server 2000/2003/2008 line.

Wolfpack shipped as MSCS with NT Server 4.0 Enterprise Edition in Sept 1997; provided failover clustering with minutes-of-downtime recovery. Renamed Windows Server Failover Clustering in Windows Server 2008.
Marathon connected Endurance 4000 at the HAL level — below normal program accessibility — to achieve transparent fault tolerance.
Software-only successor to Endurance 4000 hardware; released 2004 for Windows Server 2003; acquired by Stratus 2012.
The Endurance 4000 required matched uni-processor Pentium Pro servers as Computing Elements.
Kastner's Aberdeen had called NT Server 4.0 'a giant step forward' in July 1996; this study questions enterprise enterprise-class readiness.
BackOffice suite (Exchange, SQL Server, SNA Server, Systems Management Server) central to Microsoft's enterprise pitch. Discontinued as bundle ca. 2002.
Scalability Day claimed 1.8M emails/day with 50,000 users/server; but Kastner noted this was not yet shipping and customers reported scaling beyond a few hundred users was problematic.
Demonstrated 1 billion transactions/day and terabyte satellite imagery; Kastner critiqued benchmarks as unrealistic and TPC-A based.
100M internet hits/day benchmark on single 2-processor Pentium Pro with NT; Kastner challenged realism of 24-hour even load distribution.
Kastner's assessment: 'real enterprise high-end legitimately remains the province of Unix, the AS/400, and even the mainframe.'
Named alongside Unix and mainframe as legitimately retaining the enterprise high-end; still sold as IBM i today.
SQL Server VLM support announced as future feature; Oracle and Informix already supported on Digital Unix/Alpha for over a year.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Intel/Windows-based servers remain the dominant server platform. Windows Server 2022/2025 on Xeon processors is the mainstream enterprise server configuration.', 'source': 'General knowledge'}
column9: Both HP and Compaq offered redundant Intel/Windows server lines; major overlap area.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'legacy-supported', 'notes': 'Unix enterprise servers (AIX, HP-UX, Solaris, SPARC) are in long-term decline. Fujitsu confirmed end of Unix sales by 2029/support by 2034. Oracle Solaris, IBM AIX, and HP-UX still have active but declining installed bases with long-term support contracts.', 'source': 'https://www.techzine.eu/news/applications/73825/end-date-for-unix-systems-and-mainframe-confirmed-by-fujitsu/'}
column9: Both companies had overlapping Unix server lines.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'end-of-life', 'notes': "Intel Itanium (referred to as 'Titanium' in this collection) reached full end-of-life. Intel stopped shipments of Itanium 9700-series CPUs on July 29, 2021. HPE ended all Itanium (Integrity server) support December 31, 2025. The platform is definitively end-of-life.", 'source': 'https://www.spectrumsourcing.com/spectrum-news-feed/industry-update-hpe-to-end-itanium-support-by-december-31-2025'}
column9: Common 64-bit architecture for future Windows and Unix operating environments; both HP and Compaq working with Itanium.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Linux kernel actively developed. Dominates cloud, server, embedded, and Android markets.', 'source': 'General knowledge'}
column9: Both HP and Compaq supported Linux as additional application platform.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'High Availability as a concept and product category is fully active. Modern HA solutions include clustering, load balancing, cloud-based failover. Market growing with cloud adoption.', 'source': 'General knowledge'}
column9: Industry-leading partnerships in HA technologies cited as strength ingredient for merger success.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'Intel Itanium processor line was discontinued. Last Itanium system shipped in 2021. HP-UX and Itanium-based systems reached end of support.', 'source': 'General knowledge / Intel product announcements'}
column9: Both HP and Compaq working with Intel Itanium as common 64-bit architecture.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Dominant interconnect in HPC and AI data centers. InfiniBand switch sales surged Q2 2025 driven by NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra. NVIDIA continues manufacturing HDR/NDR InfiniBand products.', 'source': 'https://www.delloro.com/news/infiniband-switch-sales-surged-in-2q-2025-while-ethernet-maintains-market-lead-for-ai-back-end-networks/'}
column9: Serial switched fabric I/O; 1x/4x/12x (2.5/10/30 Gbps); replaces parallel PCI; HCA/TCA/switch components.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'PCI-X was superseded by PCI Express. No longer manufactured; discontinued from all modern server platforms.', 'source': 'General technology history'}
column9: Incumbent parallel I/O bus; 133 MHz; 1.06 GB/s; scaling limitations exposed by processor/network advances.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'mature', 'notes': 'Fibre Channel remains the dominant SAN protocol in enterprise storage. 32GFC and 64GFC are current generations. Mature but actively used.', 'source': 'https://www.fibrechannel.org/'}
column9: FC-based SANs; high-speed low-latency storage I/O; IBA will route FC traffic as bridge protocol.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Storage Area Networks remain active in enterprise data centers. Fibre Channel SANs (Gen 7, 64GFC) and iSCSI SANs widely deployed. Market still active though Ethernet-based storage gaining share.', 'source': 'General knowledge'}
column9: FC-based SAN; IBA router bridges IBA fabric to existing SAN; good use case for IBA deployment.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Network Attached Storage is a growing market with on-premises and cloud NAS solutions. SMB and enterprise NAS actively developed by NetApp, Synology, QNAP, Dell, and cloud providers.', 'source': 'General knowledge'}
column9: IP/Ethernet-based NAS appliances; IBA can connect NAS infrastructure via IP-over-IBA.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'legacy-supported', 'notes': 'Parallel SCSI succeeded by SAS (Serial Attached SCSI) for enterprise storage. SCSI protocol concepts live on in SAS/NVMe but parallel SCSI hardware is legacy.', 'source': 'General storage technology evolution'}
column9: Ubiquitous server-storage protocol; IBA TCA bridges parallel SCSI to IBA fabric; IBA routing hardware bridges existing SCSI.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'iSCSI is actively used for IP-based SAN storage, especially in SMB and virtualized environments. Supported by VMware, Microsoft, and all major storage vendors.', 'source': 'General storage industry'}
column9: Emerging IP-based storage protocol; cited alongside IBA as example of fabric topology trend.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'TCP/IP is the foundational protocol of the internet. Actively maintained (IPv4 and IPv6). Standard-current status. Fundamental to all internet communications.', 'source': 'https://blog.apnic.net/2025/01/13/ip-addresses-through-2024/'}
column9: IP over InfiniBand enables transparent TCP/IP over IBA fabric; IBA router offloads TCP/IP from server CPUs.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Blade server architecture remains active in enterprise data centers. While hyper-converged and cloud have taken share, blade servers (HPE Synergy, Cisco UCS) remain actively sold and supported.', 'source': 'General blade server market'}
column9: Blade servers benefit from IBA: single cable (IBA + power), dense rack, specialized blades; IBA switch in cabinet.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'succeeded', 'notes': '3GIO (Third Generation I/O) was the development codename for what became PCI Express. Formally renamed PCI Express in April 2002. Not a separate technology - it became PCIe.', 'source': 'https://news.microsoft.com/source/2002/04/17/pci-sig-introduces-pci-express-tm-formerly-3gio-high-speed-serial-interconnect-specification/'}
column9: PCI-SIG serial architecture touted as PCI replacement starting 2004; essentially became PCI Express.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'InfiniBand Host Channel Adapters (HCAs) are actively used in HPC and AI data centers. NVIDIA (formerly Mellanox) continues manufacturing HCAs for HDR/NDR InfiniBand networks.', 'source': 'https://www.delloro.com/news/infiniband-switch-sales-surged-in-2q-2025-while-ethernet-maintains-market-lead-for-ai-back-end-networks/'}
column9: Server-side IBA adapter; offloads I/O from CPU/OS; plug-in PCI card initially; embedded on system board by 2003.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'legacy-supported', 'notes': 'InfiniBand Target Channel Adapters (TCAs) were early InfiniBand components for storage targets. The TCA concept has been largely subsumed into modern HCA capabilities and storage adapter designs. The distinct TCA product category is legacy.', 'source': 'https://www.electronicdesign.com/technologies/industrial/boards/article/21772788/infiniband-chip-delivers-hca-tca-and-switch-functions'}
column9: Device-side IBA adapter; bridges IBA fabric to storage/network devices (SCSI, FC, Ethernet).

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'InfiniBand switches are actively produced and deployed for HPC and AI workloads. Sales surged in Q2 2025 driven by NVIDIA Blackwell Ultra. NVIDIA Quantum-2 NDR 400Gbps switches actively sold.', 'source': 'https://www.delloro.com/news/infiniband-switch-sales-surged-in-2q-2025-while-ethernet-maintains-market-lead-for-ai-back-end-networks/'}
column9: IBA fabric switch aggregates subnets; router bridges subnets; QoS management; also act as SCSI/FC bridges.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'TCP Offload Engines (TOE) as discrete NIC features became niche; most functions absorbed into modern SmartNIC and DPU architectures. Not a distinct product category anymore.', 'source': 'General networking technology evolution'}
column9: Offloads TCP/IP processing to IBA router; expected use case for IBA in 2004 data centers.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'AGP (Accelerated Graphics Port) was phased out 2004-2010 in favor of PCI Express. Windows 10 v1607 (2016) dropped AGP support. By 2010 no new motherboard chipsets supported AGP. Fully discontinued.', 'source': 'https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Accelerated_Graphics_Port'}
column9: PCI-based graphics interface; IBA not intended to replace AGP on system board.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Secure Digital (SD) memory cards remain the dominant removable flash storage format. SD Express (PCIe) is the current standard; microSD is ubiquitous in smartphones. SD Association actively developing new standards.', 'source': 'General knowledge'}
column9: Subject of study; portable storage for digital info transfer between devices. Key differentiator: interoperability across cameras, camcorders, printers, PCs, PDAs, DVDs. Consumer preferred capacity range 256MB-1GB.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'mature', 'notes': 'Digital camera market is mature (declining consumer, active professional). Known answer applied.', 'source': 'known'}
column9: Primary entry point to digital electronics ecosystem; owned by most focus group participants. Photos transferred primarily via USB cable rather than memory card removal. Explosive growth cited as clear market stimulant.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'Digital video camcorder as a dedicated consumer device is discontinued/absorbed by smartphones. Known answer applied.', 'source': 'known'}
column9: Most commonly owned by families raising children; footage typically transferred to VHS via cable or burned to CD.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'MP3 players as dedicated devices are discontinued. Known answer applied.', 'source': 'known'}
column9: Only a few respondents owned MP3 players in 2003; majority downloaded music to PC then burned CDs. Young people highly likely to store/use music digitally.
Palm Pilots cited; used primarily for work scheduling/directories. Only wireless digital data transfer method in use among respondents was Palm-to-Palm beam.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'Sony Memory Stick is discontinued. Known answer applied.', 'source': 'known'}
column9: Sony Memory Stick users most brand-aware among memory card users; cited as proprietary format competing with SD. SD vs. Memory Stick format war relevant context.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'SmartMedia Card is discontinued. Known answer applied.', 'source': 'known'}
column9: Referenced by one respondent (Boston male) who had SmartMedia with older Mac requiring special card reader; compatibility issues highlighted.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'legacy-supported', 'notes': 'CompactFlash is legacy-supported in niche professional use. Known answer applied.', 'source': 'known'}
column9: Memory card format variety cited as disadvantage by respondents; part of incompatible format landscape that SD aimed to resolve.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'USB ports are active. Known answer applied.', 'source': 'known'}
column9: Primary photo transfer method for most respondents; camera to PC via USB cable. Insufficient USB ports on older computers cited as pain point.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Home area network evolved to smart home. Known answer applied.', 'source': 'known'}
column9: Only a few respondents had in-home LANs; rarely used for digital data transfer. Wireless data transfer in home was essentially only Palm-to-Palm.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'CD-RW/CD-ROM as primary storage/distribution medium is discontinued. Known answer applied.', 'source': 'known'}
column9: Primary backup/archival medium for photos and music in consumer households; music downloaded then burned to CD; primary competing storage medium to memory cards.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'VHS tape is discontinued. Known answer applied.', 'source': 'known'}
column9: Primary video storage medium in consumer households in 2003; video transferred from camcorder to VHS via cable connection.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'HP Media Center PC was a Windows XP/Vista-era product line (circa 2002-2008) tied to Windows XP Media Center Edition. HP discontinued this specific product category as Windows Media Center itself was removed from Windows 10. The product line no longer exists; replaced by general-purpose home PCs and streaming devices.', 'source': 'https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZED3iwnKH2U'}
column9: Listed as qualifying device for focus group participation screening; HP-specific digital media PC platform.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Adobe Photoshop is active. Known answer applied.', 'source': 'known'}
column9: PhotoShop cited by name; photo manipulation and enhancement becoming mainstream hobby; measurable minority of consumers using it.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'P2P music download (Kazaa et al.) is discontinued as a mainstream platform. Known answer applied.', 'source': 'known'}
column9: Kazaa cited by name by San Diego IDI respondent for music downloading. Many younger respondents download music from internet though legality noted with discomfort.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'iTunes is active (evolved, split into separate apps on Mac). Known answer applied.', 'source': 'known'}
column9: iTunes referenced by Boston respondent using iCard with Firewire connection for music management; Apple music ecosystem emerging.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'Intel Centrino brand ended ~2009, replaced by Core branding. Known answer applied.', 'source': 'known'}
column9: Integrated mobile processor (mobile Pentium 4 / Pentium M), chipset, and 802.11 wireless adapter; launched March 12, 2003. Battery life >5 hours; better performance than Pentium 3/4 mobile.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Wi-Fi (802.11) is the dominant wireless networking standard. Wi-Fi 7 (802.11be) is current; Wi-Fi 6E (802.11ax) widely deployed. Wi-Fi Alliance actively certifying products.', 'source': 'General knowledge'}
column9: Centrino includes integrated 802.11 adapter; cannot be removed without cost savings; driving enterprise wireless adoption.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'discontinued', 'notes': 'Cisco LEAP (Lightweight Extensible Authentication Protocol) was deprecated due to serious security vulnerabilities (susceptible to dictionary attacks). Industry sources confirm it has been deprecated and should not be used. Superseded by PEAP, EAP-TLS, and EAP-FAST.', 'source': 'https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/computer-networks/wireless-security-part-2/'}
column9: Cisco opening LEAP to industry for wireless security/authentication; Centrino is LEAP-aware.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Wireless access points (WAPs) are active and growing infrastructure. Wi-Fi 6/6E/7 access points are current generation. The rogue AP security concern context is evergreen. Market growing significantly with 802,500 indoor small cell nodes in use in the US as of 2024.', 'source': 'https://wia.org/wireless-infrastructure-by-the-numbers-2024/'}
column9: $90 consumer APs available at CompUSA; plugged into RJ-45 LAN port; 150ft radius exposure; 'rogue AP' threat to corporate networks.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'end-of-life', 'notes': 'Windows Server 2003 reached end-of-life July 14, 2015. Known answer applied.', 'source': 'known'}
column9: Can be hardened for wireless enterprise environments; Microsoft proved on its own campus.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Public Wi-Fi hotspot infrastructure is active and growing. Global market ~$5.6B in 2024, projected CAGR of 15.4% through 2034. Wi-Fi 6 hotspots expanding significantly.', 'source': 'https://www.researchandmarkets.com/reports/6112977/wi-fi-hotspot-market-report-forecast'}
column9: Intel spending tens of millions to accelerate worldwide hotspot network where 802.11-enabled computers can access the Internet.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'succeeded', 'notes': 'Cat5 succeeded by Cat5e/Cat6. Known answer applied.', 'source': 'known'}
column9: Ubiquitous $3 cable enabling rogue AP connectivity to corporate wired LAN; cited as security vulnerability.
Hardware-based trusted computing for processors; renamed Intel TXT (Trusted Execution Technology) circa 2006
Defined as LT + OS + applications; all three required for secure computing platform
Hardware security module standard from TCG; related to LT ecosystem. TPM 2.0 became mandatory for Windows 11 in 2021
Successor name for LaGrande Technology; launched in enterprise Xeon processors circa 2006
Microsoft's trusted computing OS initiative; complementary to Intel LT. Referenced indirectly via TCG/Microsoft NDA discussions

column4: Oracle; SAP; PeopleSoft; JD Edwards; Baan; Lawson
column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Integrated ERP Application Suites (SAP S/4HANA, Oracle Fusion, Microsoft Dynamics 365) are actively developed and growing. Cloud ERP is the dominant trend.', 'source': 'General ERP market'}
column9: Fully integrated enterprise application suite; single data model; central subject of white paper; argued to deliver superior long-term ROI over point solutions.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Best-of-breed point solutions continue as a procurement strategy, particularly in MarTech and SaaS. The approach competes with integrated suite/platform approaches. Both models coexist actively.', 'source': 'General IT strategy'}
column9: Single-function enterprise software product; emerged in late 1990s; argued in paper to have higher integration costs and protracted ROI versus integrated suites.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'XML is a standard-current W3C technology. Widely used in configuration files, data exchange, and web services. Not growing for new APIs (JSON preferred) but remains broadly deployed.', 'source': 'https://www.w3.org/XML/'}
column9: Cited as most positive movement toward standardized data sharing; supported by Microsoft, Oracle, SAP, and RosettaNet/OAGI standards bodies.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Object-oriented technology (OOT) remains the dominant programming paradigm for enterprise software, frameworks, and applications. OOP is active and evolving with functional/hybrid approaches.', 'source': 'https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/object-oriented-programming-what-i-use-today-why-still-nagar-ktavc'}
column9: Object-oriented programming and component technology; cited as future promise for easier integration; seen as slow to take off but showing benefits when applied to enterprise apps.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'CRM applications are a large, growing software category. Salesforce, Microsoft Dynamics, HubSpot actively developed. Global CRM market exceeds $65B.', 'source': 'General CRM market data'}
column9: CRM as both a point solution (Siebel) and an integrated module; one of the key function areas examined in point-vs-suite debate.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Supply Chain Management applications are actively used and growing, especially post-pandemic with renewed focus on supply chain resilience. SAP, Oracle, Blue Yonder leading vendors.', 'source': 'General SCM market data'}
column9: SCM as both a point solution (i2, Manugistics) and integrated module; Nike's failed SCM/ERP/CRM integration is the central cautionary case.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'e-Procurement applications are actively developed and adopted. Market growing with digital transformation in procurement. Coupa, SAP Ariba, Jaggaer among leaders.', 'source': 'General e-procurement market'}
column9: e-Procurement cited as archetypal 'e-version of single enterprise function'; Ariba and Commerce One as point solution leaders; Ariba-i2-IBM partnership collapsed within 6 months.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Sales Force Automation (SFA) is an active software category, often integrated into broader CRM platforms. Salesforce, Microsoft, Zoho actively competing.', 'source': 'General SFA market'}
column9: Referenced as module in integrated suite requiring consistent customer ID across CRM, order entry, billing, support, and service modules.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Open APIs are fundamental to modern software architecture. API economy is growing; REST, GraphQL, and OpenAPI specification actively developed and adopted.', 'source': 'General API ecosystem'}
column9: Cited as a key integration challenge: some products have open APIs, some do not; interfaces may change at vendor's discretion, requiring new tools and retraining.

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Wi-Fi (802.11) is fully active. Wi-Fi 7 (802.11be) is current standard. Wi-Fi Alliance actively certifying products.', 'source': 'General knowledge'}
column9: 85%+ of all WiFi gear made in Taiwan and PRC; extreme supply chain concentration highlighted

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'Sony PlayStation manufacturing (and the PlayStation brand) is actively ongoing. PlayStation 5 is current generation; PlayStation 6 in development. Reference here is to semiconductor/electronics manufacturing context.', 'source': 'https://www.playstation.com/en-us/'}
column9: All PlayStation manufacturing announced to move to PRC; represents consumer electronics supply chain concentration

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': '8-inch (200mm) semiconductor fabrication plants remain in active use for mature process nodes (analog, power, RF, MEMS). The SARS-era concern about PRC-based fabs remains relevant. New 8-inch fab investments continue.', 'source': 'General semiconductor industry'}
column9: Eight new PRC 8-inch wafer fabs under construction with commitments from NEC, Philips, Oki, Elpida, Infineon

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': 'AC-to-DC power supplies are fundamental electronics components, actively manufactured and used across all electronic devices, servers, and computing equipment.', 'source': 'General electronics industry'}
column9: Invariably manufactured in PRC; example of no-second-source component dependency; laptop cannot ship without it

column7: {'lifecycle_current': 'active', 'notes': "JIT manufacturing remains widely used, though pandemic supply chain disruptions prompted shifts toward 'Just-in-Case' hybrid models. JIT is evolving with AI and real-time analytics but remains a core manufacturing strategy.", 'source': 'https://www.fictiv.com/articles/jit-just-in-time-manufacturing'}
column9: Global electronics industry supply chain model; fundamentally incompatible with quarantine/disruption scenarios
z990 latest version at study time; positioned as database server; still active as IBM Z
32-bit at study time; Intel planned 64-bit extensions; dominant server processor today

column9: Streaming media content embeddable in WebEx UCF PowerPoint presentations with full delivery control to all attendees.
Consumer OS on 50M+ aging corporate PCs; end of support January 16 2004; central subject of replacement argument
Enterprise desktop OS used as fat clients for Y2K-generation applications; end of support June 30 2003
Recommended replacement OS; described as stable with no stability problems; great client OS for desktops and laptops; EOL April 2014
Part of Windows Server 2003 ecosystem; noted for more efficient resource usage
Referenced alongside Exchange 2003 as part of new server support platform; SQL Server product line continues as active Microsoft product
Expected release in a few months from article date with new collaboration options; described as worth evaluating
Now mainstream processors; better at dealing with multi-tasking and thread-aware applications
Predicted to inaugurate 18-month platform stability period; features 800MHz front-side bus and better graphics and Ethernet processing
Wireless laptop platform featuring long battery life; about one-third of corporate PC purchases in 2003 were laptops; brand evolved and eventually retired
Referenced as even older predecessor that Y2K-era purchases replaced
Referenced alongside Windows 3.1 as legacy systems replaced during Y2K cycle
Memory-resident file appender targeting Windows 95/98/Me; discovered April 2003; provides hacker remote access via IRC; cited as evidence old software attracts attackers
Communication channel used by W95.Tenrobot virus for remote hacker access to infected computers
Core help desk/ITSM platform; AR System 3.0 released Nov 1996. Evolved into BMC Remedy ITSM Suite; still marketed by BMC as of 2024.
Aberdeen-coined category for unified internal IT operations automation; concept absorbed into ITSM and ERP categories by 2000s.
Category that spawned ITSM; Aberdeen saw help desk as launchpad for broader COM adoption.
Critical success factor in Aberdeen's COM framework; embedded in AR System v3.0.
Released November 1996; added HTML User Guide, view management, Direct SQL, improved workflow embedding.
High-density modular RAC installed in Bay 5000 MSX chassis; targeted at service providers, telcos, carriers, large enterprises. Featured BayDSP modem technology.

column2: Bay Networks Model 8000 Remote Access Concentrator
column9: Stand-alone RAC targeted at smaller ISPs and enterprise users; also features BayDSP modem technology from Penril acquisition.
Digital Signal Processing-based modem technology acquired via Penril Datability Networks; enabled 56K modem capabilities.
Competing standards x2 (3Com/US Robotics) and K56flex (Rockwell) were unified by ITU V.90 standard ratified September 1998. All dial-up modem tech obsolete post-broadband.
Modular chassis in which 5399 RAC installs; part of Bay's multi-service access product family.

column2: Bay Networks Switch Node Routing Switch
column9: Announced March 3, 1997. Layer 3 switching with dual 1.2 Gbps buses, IP AutoLearn, 5-slot chassis; wire-speed IP/IPX routing. Discontinued after Bay's acquisition by Nortel.
Bay proprietary feature; transparently integrates with existing routers without reconfiguration; automatically learns IP forwarding tables.
Combining IP/IPX routing with wire-speed hardware switching; became standard campus network design; evolved into modern multilayer switching.
Software foundation for Switch Node; same BayRS used in Bay Network routers; provided IP/IPX routing control.
2-port 100Base-TX switching module available in Switch Node hardware; Fast Ethernet becoming dominant campus backbone.

CORBA-based, object-oriented high-end dynamic web application platform; supported personalized content, CRM, knowledge management, e-commerce. Still exists as BroadVision Commerce Agility Suite in evolved form.
Low-end dynamic web server technology; Aberdeen notes poor scaling with large user loads and high database overhead.
Aberdeen predicted smart cards would bridge internet and physical retail; chip cards now universal (EMV standard) but used differently than Aberdeen envisioned.
PointCast and similar 'push' tech; Aberdeen dismissed as 'automated pull' not true personalization; category essentially disappeared by 2000.
Aberdeen's three-tier category (CGI/ODBC/CORBA) accurately described market structure; category evolved into modern web application frameworks (Django, Ruby on Rails, Node.js, etc.).
Listed as BroadVision One-to-One supported database; also Sybase 10x/11x, Informix 7x, MS SQL Server 6x.
BroadVision combines OO middleware with TP monitor technology to handle intense internet loads; key architectural differentiation.

Cabletron's market-leading network management platform; uses Inductive Modeling Technology (IMT); object-oriented distributed client/server architecture; IP for OpenSystems; major engineering investment from 1990; became Aprisma Spectrum post-Cabletron breakup; acquired by Concord then CA Technologies. Still exists as CA Spectrum as of 2020.

Policy-based VLAN and virtual networking architecture; four-phase rollout from basic switching to policy-based management; includes SecureFast SmartSwitches, VLAN Manager, Virtual Network Server; RFC 2643 documented the operational model (1999); dissolved with Cabletron reorganization.

ASIC-based switching hardware; 10M packets/second forwarding rate; up to 16,000 MAC addresses; redundant power/cooling/links; RMON support; upgrade path from shared LAN to FDDI, ATM, Fast Ethernet, Gigabit Ethernet; non-blocking switch fabric with distributed processing.

Flagship modular hub/switch chassis; integrated packet- and cell-switched backplane; 4 Gbps Internal Network Bus (INB); up to 500 ports; 10M packets/second; supports Ethernet, Token Ring, FDDI, SNA, ATM, Gigabit Ethernet; original MMAC introduced 1988 and upgradeable through all technology transitions.

Remote access product family for carrier/service provider down to SOHO markets; supports ISDN, frame relay, analog/digital modems; planned ADSL capability with Motorola Semiconductor; based on Network Express and Netlink acquisitions; Intel i960-based; dissolved with Cabletron reorganization.

Virtual LAN technology enabling logical network segmentation on shared physical infrastructure; Aberdeen notes Cabletron started VLAN work in 1990 before IEEE 802.1Q standard; IEEE 802.1Q standardized 1998; VLANs remain fundamental to modern enterprise networking.

Aberdeen's coined concept for emerging market where LAN equipment manufacturers would compete in carrier Central Office markets and vice versa; the PPSN concept partially materialized as MPLS and Metro Ethernet but was dominated by Cisco/Lucent, not Cabletron. The conceptual framework anticipated carrier-edge convergence.

Broadband access technology; Cabletron developing ADSL capability with Motorola; ADSL became mainstream broadband access technology in 2000s; now being replaced by fiber/VDSL2/cable; still deployed in some geographies.

Ipsilon's technology combining IP routing with ATM switching hardware; 5x router performance at fraction of cost; achieved via IFMP (Ipsilon Flow Management Protocol) and GSMP (Generic Switch Management Protocol); lost the standards battle to Cisco's MPLS/Tag Switching; IETF declined to standardize IFMP.

Cabletron's proprietary VLAN and policy-based network management framework; key features: connection-oriented switching, time-of-day/day-of-week access control, call-level accounting; SecureFast VLAN Operational Model published as RFC 2643 (August 1999); technology became obsolete with Cabletron's dissolution.
Protocol for managing ATM switches from IP routing layer; developed by Ipsilon; submitted to IETF; GSMP v3 eventually published as RFC 3292; technology path was superseded by MPLS.
Ipsilon's proprietary flow management protocol; core of IP Switching implementation; submitted to IETF but not adopted as standard; lost to Cisco's Tag Switching (MPLS precursor).

Cabletron's RFC 2124 protocol for policy management and accounting integration across switching platforms; one of several pre-MPLS flow management protocols that did not survive standardization process.

Cisco's competing approach to IP/ATM integration; submitted to IETF; became the basis for MPLS (Multi-Protocol Label Switching, RFC 3031); Tag Switching itself obsolete but MPLS remains fundamental to modern backbone networks.

The technology that ultimately won the IP/ATM integration standards battle; MPLS RFC 3031 published January 2001; derived from Cisco's Tag Switching; remains fundamental to carrier backbone networks, VPNs, and traffic engineering as of 2026.

Cabletron's ASIC-based switching hardware supporting VLAN, broadcast/multicast storm containment, Layer 3 virtual routing; available as modules for MMAC/MMAC-Plus chassis and standalone workgroup units; product line dissolved with Cabletron reorganization.

Aberdeen's umbrella term for front-office business applications; the category was subsequently renamed CRM (Customer Relationship Management) by Siebel, Gartner, and the analyst community; CIS as a term is now obsolete though the underlying capabilities persist in modern CRM platforms.

Subset of CIS covering lead management, opportunity tracking, account management, and sales forecasting; Aberdeen identifies SFA as the most mature CIS module at time of study; remains a core module of all modern CRM platforms.

Enabling technology linking phone systems to CRM/help desk software; Aberdeen identifies CTI as a critical CIS enabler; the technology evolved into modern UCaaS and contact-center-as-a-service (CCaaS) platforms.

Shift from client-server to internet-based CIS deployment; Aberdeen dedicates a chapter to this transition; became the dominant architecture by 2002-2005; the web paradigm eventually gave way to SaaS (Salesforce model, 1999+).

Aberdeen identifies CIS-ERP integration as a 'rapidly emerging trend'; proved highly accurate — became a major driver of SAP CRM, Oracle Siebel integration, and eventually the ERP-CRM convergence in cloud suites.
Mid-market SFA product; passed through Interact Commerce, Sage, Swiftpage; rebranded as Infor CRM then Act! Premium for Web; significantly diminished market position.
Industry-leading CRM suite; dominated market by 2000; acquired by Oracle (2005); Oracle Siebel CRM remains as on-premises legacy product; Oracle Fusion/CX is successor.
Help desk/ITSM workflow platform; Remedy ARS became BMC Remedy ITSM after 2002 acquisition; still actively used in enterprise environments as BMC Helix ITSM.

Not in study (founded 1999, post-report); included for context as the disruptive force that reshaped the entire CIS/CRM market Aberdeen was analyzing. Salesforce's SaaS model eventually displaced most vendors profiled in this report.

The de facto standard internetworking operating system; described by Aberdeen as 'unifying thread' providing cohesion to disparate networks; four cornerstones: scalability, WAN optimization, management/security, reliable adaptive routing; IOS remained dominant through 2000s; evolved into IOS XE, IOS XR for modern platforms; still the basis for Cisco's networking OS.

Cisco's per-flow packet forwarding and statistics technology; implemented in IOS 11 (1996); establishes flow cache to speed packet processing; exports per-conversation statistics; provides QoS and accounting capabilities; Aberdeen notes 10% processing overhead tradeoff; NetFlow v9 became basis for IETF IPFIX standard (RFC 7011, 2013); still widely used for network monitoring as of 2026.

Cisco's next-generation router designed for ISPs and internet backbone; first deployed 1997; multigigabit crossbar fabric sustaining 80 Gbps; interfaces OC-3/OC-12/OC-48, Gigabit Ethernet, Packet over SONET, ATM; Aberdeen described as 'appropriate and necessary'; sold 1,000 units by November 1998; became a major commercial success; still referenced in carrier networks.

High-end ATM broadband switch from StrataCom acquisition; first integration point for Tag Switching and Cisco IOS; BXM Broadband Switching Modules based on Stratm Technology; key to Cisco's ISP market positioning; eventually superseded by IP/MPLS platforms.

High-performance ASIC set for ATM switching and traffic management from StrataCom; features complete ABR support, virtual path trunking, weighted fair queuing, multicast; enables higher-density lower-cost ATM switching; integrated into BPX platforms.

Aberdeen-era workhorse enterprise and service provider router; target for NetFlow and Tag Switching capabilities; VIP2 (Versatile Interface Processor 2) enhanced with high-speed interfaces; 4x capacity increase through new processors; eventually superseded by 7600, ASR 1000, and 12000 GSR series.

Enterprise internet commerce platform; components: transaction engine (Financial eXchange), application portfolio (Catalog, Digital Delivery, Chat, Note), Oasis distributed architecture, Workplace management tools; EDI integration driver; CORBA and DCOM neutral via abstraction layer; clients included Broderbund, MicroWarehouse, Multiple Zones, Comdisco, Scholastic.

column2: Oasis (Object Architecture and Scalable Interoperating System)
column9: InterWorld's distributed transaction processing platform; abstraction layer between applications and object architectures (CORBA/DCOM); avoids technology lock-in; remote object marshaling to minimize network communication; eliminates universal messaging queue bottlenecks; dissolved with InterWorld.

Microsoft's distributed object model for Windows-based internet commerce; competing with CORBA for enterprise internet commerce standard; InterWorld's Oasis supports DCOM without translation gateways; DCOM largely obsolete, superseded by WCF, REST, and cloud APIs.

Electronic document interchange for B2B transactions (purchase orders, invoices); Fortune 1000 standard for supply chain integration; Commerce eXchange supports EDI via driver for B2B channel; still used in legacy supply chain systems but being replaced by API-based integration and EDI-over-AS2/AS4.

Aberdeen notes Commerce eXchange uses SSL to protect sessions between browser and server; RC4 and MD5 encryption; SSL deprecated in favor of TLS (Transport Layer Security); TLS 1.3 is current standard; SSL itself obsolete.

InterWorld's digital goods download component; compliant with Microsoft ESD guidelines and SPA EULA; integrates with software clearinghouses; precursor concept to modern app stores (App Store 2008, Steam, etc.).

The emerging category of software that Commerce eXchange exemplified; Aberdeen's analysis of this category anticipates modern e-commerce platforms (Shopify, Magento, Salesforce Commerce Cloud, etc.); the category grew to a multi-billion dollar market.
RS/6000 SP evolved into IBM eServer pSeries 690; eventually replaced by IBM Power servers
DataBlade technology absorbed into IBM after Informix acquisition in 2001
TCP/IP became universal networking standard
OLTP remains a core enterprise workload category
Data warehousing evolved into cloud data warehouses; Snowflake, BigQuery etc dominant by 2020s
N-tiered client-server CRM suite including Sales Enterprise and Service Enterprise; acquired by Oracle 2006; continues as Oracle Siebel CRM
Flagship SFA product; contact manager, activity manager, scheduler, calendar, EIS reporting; version 3.0 released February 1997
Customer support module; service request logging, escalation, contract management, online knowledge base
Multi-tier application architecture separating presentation, business logic, and data; became standard enterprise architecture pattern
Software automating sales processes; core market for Siebel; became part of broader CRM category
Broad category managing customer lifecycle; Siebel helped define and dominate this market
Integration of phone systems with CRM data; Siebel 3.0 integrated with Prospect and Genesys CTI middleware
Graphical configuration tool introduced in Siebel v3.0 allowing customization without source code modification
Siebel's development platform; contributed to Windows-centric deployment; MFC largely superseded by .NET
Siebel anticipated pen computing for field sales; evolved into mobile CRM on smartphones and tablets
Joint product combining EMC intelligent storage with BMC data movement software; bypassed mainframe scheduling constraints
Extracting data from mainframe systems (IBM z/OS) for use in open systems environments; still practiced in large enterprises
Central repository for integrated data supporting decision making; term popularized by Bill Inmon in early 1990s
Subject-specific subsets of data warehouses for departmental or functional use
Unix and Windows-based computing environments contrasted with proprietary mainframe systems; now largely cloud and x86 Linux
DMTF standard for desktop PC management; DMI 2.0 released June 1998; end-of-life declared March 31 2005; superseded by CIM and WMI
HP's flagship enterprise PC line; first DMI-compliant PCs (1992); discontinued 2002 after HP-Compaq merger; replaced by HP Evo
DMI-compliant desktop management suite; included AssetTOOLS, ConfigTOOLS, FlashTOOL, SafeTOOLS, ProtectTOOLS; discontinued with Vectra line
HP's network and systems management platform; rebranded as HP Software Division products in 2007; sold to MicroFocus; now part of OpenText
Remote PC power-on using Magic Packet technology co-developed with AMD; predecessor of Wake-on-LAN standard
Remote power-on technology; evolved into Wake-on-LAN (WOL) standard; still widely used in enterprise PC management
Compaq's proprietary desktop management system; required Compaq hardware and invited third-party partners; discontinued after HP acquisition of Compaq 2002
Hard drive health monitoring technology; still widely used in modern storage devices
DMTF standard that superseded DMI; foundation for WMI and modern IT management
Microsoft's implementation of CIM/WBEM that became the dominant desktop management standard; shipped with Windows 2000

Complete re-architecture of BusinessObjects; integrated Reporter, Explorer, Document Agent Server, Designer, Supervisor modules; foundation for subsequent releases; became SAP BusinessObjects after 2008
Client-side multidimensional analysis engine; builds compact microcubes on-the-fly from SQL queries; eliminates pre-built cube requirement; concept evolved into modern in-memory analytics
Business representation of database hiding SQL complexity; drag-and-drop metaphor for IS and business users; concept lives on in SAP HANA and modern BI semantic layers
Three-tier web application server to bring BusinessObjects functionality to WWW via Java applets; became WebIntelligence upon release; concept evolved into web-based BI standard
Desktop decision-tree data mining tool licensed from ISoft; $995 standalone or $495 with BusinessObjects 4.0; discontinued; data mining integrated into broader BI platforms
Third-tier component for scheduled report updating and distribution; $4,995; concept evolved into modern BI server and report scheduling
Multidimensional data analysis; BusinessObjects 4.0 brought OLAP to mainstream users without proprietary OLAP databases
Pattern discovery in large datasets; Aberdeen predicted democratization of data mining from specialists to business analysts; prediction proved accurate by 2010s
OLAP analysis on top of relational databases; BusinessObjects schemaless approach allowed this without star/snowflake schema requirements
Project Darwin planned to use Java applets for web delivery of BI; Java applets deprecated by browsers by 2017; concept succeeded through HTML5 and modern web standards
Broad category for analytics and reporting tools; Business Objects was a founding vendor of the category
NT-based S.100 CT resource management platform. Succeeded by IP telephony middleware (SIP, H.323). Eventually subsumed into Intel's acquisition and later Dialogic Group's product portfolio.
Object-oriented C-language media processing API for CT servers. Largely superseded by VoIP/SIP standards in early 2000s.
Windows telephony application programming interface. Remained relevant in enterprise telephony through 2000s; superseded by VoIP APIs.
Hardware bus interoperability standard for CT boards; replaced SC Bus and MVIP. Superseded by packet-switched VoIP architectures.
Time-division multiplexing hardware bus for CT boards. Predated H.100.
Competing CT hardware bus standard. Both MVIP and SC Bus were superseded by H.100.
Telephony Services Application Programming Interface for NetWare. Competed with TAPI. Declined with NetWare market share.
Competitor architecture to CT Media announced June 1997; S.100 support not expected until 1998. Company was acquired by EAS Group in 2005 and folded into Cantata.
S.300 SPI hardware interface and S.400 object-oriented API were proposed extensions to S.100. Never achieved significant adoption before VoIP superseded the ECTF framework.
ARM-based processor; 233 MHz delivering highest Java CaffeineMark scores. Sold to Intel in 1997 patent settlement; Intel shipped SA-1100 in 1998; succeeded by XScale (2000).
Comprehensive NC software: NC Server, NC Access ROM OS, NC Card smartcard, NC Applications. Discontinued in 1999 when NCI was shut down.
Standard hardware specification for Network Computers. Attracted IBM, Sun. Concept succeeded by thin-client and cloud standards.
Intel/Microsoft response to NC; restricted expansion slots, remote management. Did not achieve significant market adoption; superseded by modern managed PCs.
Central to NC concept. Java performance of StrongARM was a key NC selling point. Java remains active under Oracle ownership.
ISO-standard smart card for NC user authentication. Concept was ahead of its time; similar approaches now in use (PIV, FIDO2).
BSD Unix-based NC server OS for small businesses. Discontinued with NCI shutdown.
64-bit Unix OS for Alpha servers. Continued as Tru64 UNIX under Compaq/HP until support ended ~2012.
64-bit RISC processor architecture; key DEC differentiator. IP sold to Intel 2001; last Alpha systems sold by HP until 2007.
DIGITAL's Telecommunications Management Platform; supports TMN standards; proactive fault detection. Product continued under HP/Compaq as HP OpenView extensions.
Enterprise telecom billing platform. 50% faster on Alpha vs other platforms per benchmark. Kenan acquired by Lucent 1999; Amdocs acquired Kenan billing assets from Lucent in 2015.
Store-and-forward SMS text messaging platform; European GSM standard support. Became foundational to global SMS ecosystem. EMI protocol used by CMG.
Software routing phone calls over IP/Intranet to eliminate toll charges. TAPI-compatible. Acquired by Nokia 1998; technology integrated into Nokia gateway products.
US version of GSM at 1900 MHz band. CMG targeted PCS 1900 for SMS services. Succeeded by 3G/4G/5G standards.
High-end symmetric multiprocessing Alpha server used in telco deployments. Succeeded by Compaq/HP server lines.
DEC's proprietary OS; ran on Alpha. Still maintained by VMS Software Inc. (VSI) under HP license; used in niche mission-critical deployments.
Flagship Alpha SMP server; 12-CPU configuration; Alpha 5/300 or 5/440 processor. Succeeded by Compaq AlphaServer GS series; discontinued after HP acquisition.
Alpha processors cited: 5/300, 5/440, 5/466. Last Alpha processor (EV79) canceled after HP acquisition. Alpha IP sold to Intel 2001.
Crossbar-switch interconnect clustering using Memory Channel technology contributed by Encore Computer. Enabled low-overhead SMP cluster expansion. Discontinued with DEC/Alpha.
64-bit Unix for Alpha; Very Large Memory support. Continued as Tru64 UNIX under Compaq/HP. Support ended ~2012.
600 MHz Alpha workstation starting at $10,000; dual OS support (Unix + NT); PowerStorm graphics. Discontinued with DEC acquisition.
DEC's 2D/3D graphics accelerator; OpenGL support; PCI bus. Discontinued with DEC acquisition.
Leading-edge Win95/NT Fortran compiler for Alpha and Intel. Technology later evolved into Intel Visual Fortran (after DEC semiconductor acquisition).
Automatic parallelization preprocessor for SMP servers. Technology evolved into Intel KAP products.
Industry-standard library for message-passing parallel applications. Still foundational to HPC; MPI-4.0 standardized 2021.
High-level language for parallel cluster applications. Declined in favor of MPI/OpenMP; never widely adopted in production.
IBM's MPP architecture; Aberdeen criticized as overly expensive for typical HPTC. Evolved into IBM Blue Gene and Power Systems; defeated Kasparov in chess (Deep Blue).
MIPS-based Unix workstations. Aberdeen noted SGI's lack of NT transition commitment. SGI discontinued IRIX hardware; SGI went bankrupt 2009.
DEC's super-fast backup/restore solution; 400-750 GB/hour; AlphaServer 8400 + tape automation. Discontinued with DEC acquisition by Compaq in 1998.
8-CPU Alpha server used as NonStop VLDB backup server hardware. Benchmark configuration: 8 CPUs, 8 GB RAM, 30 SCSI controllers, 16 tape drives.
DEC's high-speed I/O channel supporting 4 PCI buses; key enabler of NonStop VLDB performance.
The 'major backup technology advance of the last 10 years' per Aberdeen in 1997. Evolved into SAN snapshots, deduplication, and cloud backup.
SAP R/3 enterprise application compatible with NonStop VLDB; backup results 400-750 GB/hour. Succeeded by SAP S/4HANA.
Tested with NonStop VLDB. Legato acquired by EMC 2003; NetWorker continues as Dell Technologies NetWorker.
Tested with NonStop VLDB. Cheyenne acquired by CA 1996; ARCserve continues as Arcserve (spun out 2019).
OS for AlphaServer NonStop VLDB configurations. Support ended ~2012.
Technology that Aberdeen's vision of fast backup/restore foreshadowed. Data Domain (EMC) pioneered deduplication in early 2000s; now ubiquitous in backup.
The ultimate realization of Aberdeen's 'backup goes strategic' vision; cloud backup/DR as service now standard enterprise practice.
Broad category encompassing CORBA and DCOM; succeeded by web services and microservices
CORBA became popular in mid-1990s then declined after early 2000s; still used in legacy telecom and defense systems
Microsoft dropped DCOM after failed attempts to scale; replaced by .NET Remoting then WCF then REST APIs
Core CORBA runtime component; niche use in legacy systems today
The Internet application platform the report identified as the key driver did indeed become dominant
New product at time of study; combined GUI workflow model for warehouse design/construction/maintenance; absorbed into Ardent DataStage
Metadata directory for technical and business users; CDIF integration with CASE tools; absorbed into Ardent/Informix/IBM
Data warehouse implementation methodology based on 250 engagements; ~30 modules; superseded by agile DW practices
Java/HTML views of metadata for browser-enabled users; $2500 for 5 concurrent seats; early web BI capability
Business user personal data mart tool; point-and-click interface; precursor to modern self-service BI
Data warehouse architecture central to study; matured into modern data platforms cloud data warehouses (Snowflake Redshift BigQuery)
Departmental data marts proliferating in 1997; became standard BI delivery pattern; modern equivalent is data lakehouse subject areas
Aberdeen identifies metadata as the key to DW scalability; modern data catalogs (Collibra Alation Atlan) validate this insight
Prism planned CORBA integration for metadata distribution; CORBA was superseded by web services then REST
Prism planned DCOM integration alongside CORBA; both superseded
CDIF used for metadata exchange between Prism and CASE tools/repositories; deprecated as standards evolved
Prism feature for reusing mappings tables user exits; concept evolved into modern ETL reusable transformation patterns
The ETL paradigm Prism implements became the standard data integration pattern; evolved to ELT for cloud
2-week on-site consulting and training program for Iterations methodology; absorbed into Ardent/Informix services
Absorbed into Security Dynamics/RSA Security after July 1997 acquisition; technology elements survived in RSA portfolio
SSO became foundational enterprise security architecture; modern implementations via SAML OAuth OIDC (Okta Ping Identity Microsoft AD)
RSA algorithm remained industry standard for asymmetric encryption; RSA Data Security acquired by Security Dynamics 1996
X.509 became the universal standard for digital certificates; used in TLS/HTTPS today
Supported Windows 3.1 Windows 95 Windows NT desktop; transparent SSO and disk encryption
Pre-tested application connectors for Oracle Sybase Informix Unix networking; absorbed after acquisition
Central authorization server for Unix platforms (Digital HP-UX AIX Solaris); NT Server support in field test as of study
APIs and libraries for integrating custom applications with BoKS; used by DynaSoft for BoKS Connect module production
Software-based credential container independent of specific credential implementation; concept evolved into modern software tokens
Common Data Interchange Format; used by BoKS for metadata exchange with CASE tools and repositories; now deprecated
HP's SSO product incorporating DynaSoft BoKS technology; discontinued as HP security portfolio evolved
Sun's SSO/security management product using DynaSoft as strategic partner; discontinued after Oracle acquisition of Sun
EDI identified as established EC foundation; still used in legacy supply chains but largely replaced by web-based B2B integration
Aberdeen's term for fusion of EC and Internet technologies; became the dominant paradigm for all commerce by 2005
EC automation wave enabled by Internet; confirmed by explosive growth of online retail B2B commerce
Virtual distribution channels Aberdeen describes as disrupting business equilibrium; now the primary sales channel for many industries
The 'rapid adoption of Internet technologies' Aberdeen cites as enabler of IC revolution; confirmed as dominant infrastructure
Core HADE product. Acquired by Sun 1999; evolved into Sun Forte Developer; discontinued ~2002.
Upper-CASE design and code generation tool integrated with Forte.
Production workflow engine for automating business rules in distributed applications.
Object Request Broker protocol. Declined with rise of Web Services (SOAP/REST) in early 2000s.
Distributed Computing Environment Remote Procedure Call. Superseded by DCOM then web services.
Category coined to distinguish enterprise-grade RAD tools from simpler tools. Category faded as Java IDEs dominated.
Technique to split apps across client/server tiers. Concept evolved into modern microservices architecture.
IBM transaction processing monitor supported by Forte for high-volume OLTP.
Evolved to Windows 2000 Server, Windows Server 2003/2008/2012/2019/2022. Became dominant enterprise OS.
HP/Microsoft joint strategy announced March 1997. Included TCO reduction, joint application solutions, heterogeneous interoperability. Dissolved when HP acquired Compaq in 2002.
HP planned to port OpenView IT/Operations to NT by year-end 1997 as part of SEC. Evolved into HP Software; renamed to Micro Focus Operations Bridge after 2017 Micro Focus acquisition.
HP endorsed Exchange as strategic NT messaging; HP OpenMail to provide migration services. Exchange remains a major Microsoft product.
HP co-developed Merced with Intel; expected early access advantage. Itanium shipped 2001; discontinued 2021 after failing to displace x86-64.
HP endorsed Wolfpack as strategic NT HA clustering. Wolfpack became Microsoft Cluster Service (MSCS) in Windows 2000; now Windows Server Failover Clustering.
Tandem's hardware clustering architecture for NT high-end. Post-Compaq acquisition, evolved into HPE NonStop systems.
HP VPA allowed bundled discounts across printers, networking, servers. Aberdeen cited as major competitive advantage over Compaq.
HP's internal PC management environment saving $200M/year on 100,000+ employees. Being made externally available as part of SEC.
Low-cost NetPC at $1,000 entry-level announced as part of SEC. Network PC concept faded as thin clients and standard PCs dominated.
Announced May 1997; shipped November 1997. HP 9000 line discontinued 2003; succeeded by HP Integrity (Itanium).
15.3 GB/sec crossbar; derived from Convex supercomputing tech; operated at 120 MHz over 64-bit path.
PA-8200 in initial V2200; PA-8500 in 1998 upgrade. PA-RISC succeeded by Itanium (IA-64) in 2001.
HP planned NUMA extension for 1999 V-Class beyond 32-way SMP. NUMA became standard in modern multi-socket servers.
HP anticipated Merced integration for 1999 V-Class. Merced (Itanium) shipped 2001, not 1999. Itanium ultimately failed to displace x86-64; discontinued 2021.
Hybrid SMP/MPP cluster; EPS40 = up to 16 V2200 nodes (256 processors). Discontinued with HP 9000 line.
V2200 required HP-UX 11.0 (released November 1997). HP-UX still supported on HP Integrity but no new development.
Decision support benchmark cited for competitive comparisons. Superseded by TPC-H.
Aberdeen dismissed NT for enterprise production workloads in 1997. NT evolved to Windows 2000/Server 2003 which gained significant enterprise traction.
VisualAge family discontinued April 30, 2007. Eclipse framework derived from VisualAge Micro Edition (donated by IBM 2001). VisualAge Smalltalk transferred to Instantiations as VA Smalltalk in 2005.
Upcoming at time of study (1997). VisualAge for Java evolved; Eclipse replaced it. VisualAge ME became Eclipse framework donated to open source 2001.
FlowMark graphical workflow modeling and execution. Evolved into IBM MQ Workflow; later IBM Business Process Manager (BPM).
Group development and repository management. Succeeded by IBM Rational ClearCase and other Rational products post-2003 acquisition.
Cross-platform application generation from platform-independent specifications. Evolved into IBM RDi (Rational Developer for i); still available for IBM i (AS/400 successor).
System Object Model / Distributed SOM. IBM's CORBA-based object sharing. Competed with Microsoft DCOM. Discontinued; DCOM won the desktop, CORBA/J2EE won enterprise middleware.
IBM/Apple compound document component architecture. Abandoned 1997 by Apple; IBM discontinued soon after. Lost to Microsoft OLE/ActiveX.
Conceptual modeling and physical data design tool for performance optimization. Superseded by IBM Data Studio and other tools.
Graphical business process mapping. Evolved into IBM WebSphere Business Modeler and eventually IBM Business Process Manager.
Middleware for scaling data-intensive Internet applications via comms multiplexing and load-balancing. Evolved into WebSphere Application Server transaction services.
Enterprise-scale data warehouse built on DB2. Evolved into IBM DB2 Warehouse; superseded by IBM Analytics/Cognos post-2007 Cognos acquisition.
10-100GB departmental data marts. Focused on fast installation; targeted OS/2 and Windows NT environments. Superseded by IBM DB2 Data Warehouse Manager.
Evolution of Metaphor DIS; OLAP analytical application server. Discontinued; Cognos PowerPlay/TM1 became IBM's OLAP platform post-2007.

Data mining supporting clustering, classification, prediction, association, sequential pattern discovery. IBM discontinued Intelligent Miner ~2010; capabilities absorbed into SPSS Modeler (IBM acquired SPSS 2009).
RDBMS change capture and replication for data warehouse population. Evolved into IBM InfoSphere Data Replication / IBM Data Replication.
Federated data access across heterogeneous databases. Evolved into IBM WebSphere Federation Server / IBM InfoSphere Federation Server.
Web browser interface to DB2; key Internet-enablement tool for BI Family. Discontinued ~2005 as JSP/servlets became standard.
Core underlying RDBMS for all IBM BI Family products. DB2 remains an active IBM product (IBM Db2 family).
Information catalog allowing end users to browse business-view metadata before drilling down. Concept evolved into modern data catalogs (IBM InfoSphere Information Governance Catalog).
IBM systems management framework integrated with BI Family. Evolved into IBM Tivoli (now IBM Automation).

column7: Active (renamed IBM MQ 2014; v9.4 released June 2024)
column9: Launched 1993; renamed WebSphere MQ 2002; renamed IBM MQ 2014. Active as of 2026. One of the most durable enterprise middleware products.

column7: Active (CICS TS for z/OS 6.3 released September 2025)
column9: Introduced 1968; still actively developed for z/OS. CICS for IBM i sunsetted 2024-2025. Core z/OS product remains vibrant.

column138: Software layer acting as a traffic cop directing requests between distributed client and server systems; maintains a directory/repository of objects and their interfaces.
column141: ORB/CORBA-based middleware largely supplanted by web services (SOAP/REST), ESBs, and microservices architectures. Still used in legacy enterprise environments.

column138: Digital Equipment Corporation's ORB implementation; version 2.5 at time of study. Supports 19 hardware/OS combinations. Evolved from ACA Services offering.
column141: Product discontinued following Digital's acquisition by Compaq in 1998. Superseded by DCOM/COM+ and later web services.

column138: OMG standard for distributed object interoperability using Interface Definition Language (IDL). ObjectBroker conforms to CORBA 1.2 at time of study.
column141: CORBA remains technically active in some sectors (finance, defense, telecoms via IONA/Micro Focus) but is broadly considered legacy middleware displaced by REST/SOAP/gRPC.

column138: Language-neutral specification language used to define object interfaces in CORBA. ObjectBroker 2.5 supports IDL bindings to C and a Visual Basic DLL.
column141: IDL persists within CORBA/OMG standards and in some API description tools, but mainstream use is supplanted by interface description formats like OpenAPI and Protocol Buffers.

column138: Digital extension to IDL describing characteristics of each object implementation.
column141: Proprietary Digital extension; discontinued with ObjectBroker product.

column138: Digital extension to IDL specifying how to select among multiple implementations of an object.
column141: Proprietary Digital extension; discontinued with ObjectBroker product.

column138: Microsoft standard for compound documents and inter-application communication. ObjectBroker 2.5 provides OLE Network Portal for OLE-CORBA integration.
column141: OLE evolved into OLE 2.0, then ActiveX/COM, then COM+, and ultimately .NET component model. Legacy OLE automation still used in some Office integration scenarios.

column138: Microsoft binary interface standard underlying OLE. Defines how software components interact. CORBA and COM coexist in ObjectBroker strategy.
column141: COM evolved into COM+/.NET. COM interop still supported in .NET but COM itself is legacy for new development. Windows Runtime (WinRT) is the modern successor.

column138: Older Microsoft inter-process communication protocol. ObjectBroker 2.5 includes a DDE Gateway/Listener for legacy DDE-aware servers.
column141: DDE largely deprecated since Windows 2000 era; succeeded by COM/OLE and DCOM. Some legacy automation tools still use it.

column138: OSF/DCE standard for distributed computing security (GSS API). ObjectBroker 2.6 roadmap includes GSS API for DCE to enable Kerberos-level security.
column141: DCE largely obsolete; superseded by Kerberos directly, LDAP, and modern identity/auth frameworks such as OAuth2 and OpenID Connect.

column138: Microsoft RAD tool mentioned as a front-end application development option for ORB-based architectures. ObjectBroker 2.5 supports a Visual Basic DLL IDL binding.
column141: Visual Basic evolved into VB.NET (introduced 2002 with .NET Framework). Classic VB 6.0 development ended; VB.NET remains supported but is niche relative to C#.

column138: Sybase (then Appeon) RAD development tool mentioned as a front-end option for building OLE-aware client applications against CORBA backends.
column141: PowerBuilder persists in niche enterprise markets under Appeon ownership (acquired from SAP in 2014); new development is minimal but legacy applications remain.

column138: Digital's VMS operating system for both VAX and Alpha hardware. Supported by ObjectBroker on both architectures.
column141: OpenVMS still supported under VSI (VMS Software Inc), which licensed OpenVMS from HP (later HPE) and continues active development for x86-64 in addition to Alpha and Integrity.

column138: CORBA 2.0 IDL-to-C++ binding planned for ObjectBroker 2.6; would allow native C++ development environment.
column141: C++ remains an active systems programming language; CORBA C++ binding (OMG IDL-to-C++) is legacy but C++ itself is widely used.

column138: Standard API for security services. ObjectBroker 2.6 roadmap includes GSS API for DCE enabling Kerberos-level authentication.
column141: GSS-API/Kerberos remains active and widely deployed (Active Directory, MIT Kerberos, Heimdal). The API standard itself is still relevant.

column138: Digital's predecessor distributed application integration offering that evolved into ObjectBroker.
